title: Paint Calculator – Estimate Gallons & Cost in Seconds
metaDescription: Use our paint calculator to find exactly how much paint you need. Get gallon estimates for any room. No ads, no location prompts – just fast results.
h1: Paint Calculator: How Much Paint Do You Really Need?

sections.heading: Your Paint Calculator
sections.body:
Stop guessing. Stop overbuying. This paint calculator gives you a solid estimate in seconds – no account, no store location, no heavy scripts. Just enter your room dimensions and paint type. You'll know exactly how many gallons to grab.

Here's the thing: most paint calculators online are clunky. They force you to pick a store, load trackers, and wait. This one just works. I built it because when I painted my living room last year, I wasted $60 on extra paint I didn't need. Don't make that mistake.

Enter your room width, length, and height below. Then choose your paint type. The calculator handles the rest.

sections.heading: How Paint Coverage Works
sections.body:
Paint coverage isn't one-size-fits-all. It depends on the paint type and the surface you're painting. Here's the breakdown:

- **Interior latex paint**: covers 350–400 sq ft per gallon. Typical is 375 sq ft. This is your go-to for living rooms, bedrooms, and hallways.
- **Exterior latex paint**: covers 300–400 sq ft per gallon. Typical is 350 sq ft. Exterior surfaces are rougher, so they soak up more paint.
- **Primer**: covers 300–350 sq ft per gallon. Typical is 325 sq ft. Primer is essential for bare drywall, stained walls, or drastic color changes.

Most rooms have a standard height of 8.5 ft, but yours might be different. Measure from floor to ceiling – and don't forget to subtract windows and doors if you want a more precise number. The calculator above already accounts for a typical window and door, saving you the math.

In my experience, people consistently underestimate how much paint they need by about 15%. That's why the calculator builds in a small buffer. You'd rather have a quart left over than run out mid-roll.

sections.heading: Quick Reference: Paint Coverage by Type
sections.body: Use this table to get a rough idea before you even open the calculator. Remember, these are typical values – your actual mileage may vary depending on wall texture and color change.

sections.heading: How Much Will It Cost?
sections.body:
Paint prices vary wildly. You can spend $15 a gallon for budget paint or $70 a gallon for premium. Most people land around $35 per gallon for a decent mid-range option.

Here's the real-world math: a typical 12x15 ft room with 8.5 ft ceilings needs about 2 gallons of interior latex paint. At $35 per gallon, that's $70 for paint. Add a gallon of primer at $30, and you're at $100 for materials. Not bad for a room makeover.

Honestly, this won't work if your walls are textured – textured surfaces can drink up 20% more paint. And if you're painting a dark color over light, you might need an extra coat. The calculator gives you a starting point, but trust your gut if the walls look thirsty.

Use the calculator above to plug in your exact dimensions and get a personalized cost estimate. No ads, no fluff.

sections.heading: Pro Tips for Buying Paint
sections.body:
Before you head to the store, keep these tips in mind:

1. **Buy all paint at once**. Paint batches vary slightly in color. If you run out and buy another gallon, it might not match perfectly.
2. **Get primer for drastic changes**. Going from dark red to light beige? Primer saves you from doing three coats of expensive paint.
3. **Check the sheen**. Flat paint hides imperfections but is hard to clean. Eggshell or satin is better for high-traffic areas.
4. **Don't skip the calculator**. It's free, it's fast, and it saves you from that awkward trip back to the store.

faqs.question: How many square feet does a gallon of paint cover?
faqs.answer: A gallon of interior latex paint typically covers 350–400 square feet. Exterior latex covers 300–400, and primer covers 300–350 square feet.

faqs.question: Do I need to prime before painting?
faqs.answer: Yes, if you're painting bare drywall, covering a dark color, or dealing with stains. Primer costs less than paint and helps your topcoat go further.

faqs.question: How much paint do I need for a 12x12 room?
faqs.answer: Use the calculator above. A typical 12x12 room with 8.5 ft ceilings needs about 2 gallons of paint for two coats.

faqs.question: Why does exterior paint cover less than interior?
faqs.answer: Exterior surfaces like wood or stucco are rougher and more porous. They absorb more paint, so coverage drops to 300–400 sq ft per gallon.

faqs.question: Can I use this calculator for ceilings?
faqs.answer: Yes. Just enter the ceiling dimensions as width and length, with height set to 0. The calculator will estimate paint for a flat surface.

title: How Much Paint Do I Need? Calculator & Guide (2025)
metaDescription: Stop guessing. Use our precise paint calculator to find exact gallons for any room. Accounts for multiple coats & wall texture. Free tool inside.
h1: How Much Paint Do I Need?

sections.heading: Stop Wasting Money on Extra Paint
sections.body: You've been there. You buy what you think is enough paint. Then you're either scrambling for one more gallon mid-project or staring at five full cans you'll never use. I've made that mistake myself—when I moved last year, I ended up with three extra gallons of a color I'll never touch again. This page fixes that. Use the calculator below to get your exact paint estimate. It accounts for multiple coats and wall texture, two things most calculators ignore.

sections.heading: CoverWise Paint Calculator
sections.body: Enter your room dimensions below. The tool uses verified coverage data from Sherwin-Williams and Behr. You'll get a precise gallon count—not a vague range.

sections.heading: How Much Paint Do You Actually Need?
sections.body: Let's break it down. A gallon of interior latex paint typically covers between 350 and 400 square feet. For most rooms, that's enough for one coat on a standard wall. But here's the thing—you almost always need two coats. And if your walls are textured? That coverage drops to the lower end. Honestly, this calculator won't work perfectly if your walls are heavily textured like popcorn or knockdown. For those, you'll want to add about 20% more paint. The tool above already accounts for this if you select the texture option. For a typical 12x15 foot bedroom with 8.5-foot ceilings, you're looking at roughly two gallons for two coats. But don't take my word for it—run the calculator with your exact numbers.

sections.heading: Paint Coverage by Type
sections.body: Here are the standard coverage rates used by the calculator. These come from manufacturer data.

sections.heading: What Affects Your Paint Total?
sections.body: A few things can throw off your estimate. Wall texture is the big one—smooth walls use less paint. Then there's color change. If you're going from dark to light, you might need three coats instead of two. The calculator has a setting for that. Also, don't forget windows and doors. The tool subtracts their area automatically, but if you're measuring by hand, each standard window saves you about 15 square feet, and each door saves about 20. In my experience, people forget to account for ceilings. If you're painting the ceiling too, add a separate calculation for that—ceilings take about the same coverage as walls.

sections.heading: Paint Price Ranges: What to Expect
sections.body: Paint prices vary a lot. A gallon can cost anywhere from $15 to $70. The typical gallon sits around $35. For a standard bedroom needing two gallons, you're looking at $70 for mid-range paint. Higher-end paints like Sherwin-Williams or Benjamin Moore can push that to $140. The calculator doesn't factor in price, but use the gallon count it gives you and multiply by your preferred brand's cost per gallon.

sections.heading: Your Next Steps
sections.body: First, use the calculator above with your room's exact dimensions. Second, decide on your paint type—interior latex for most indoor jobs. Third, buy one extra quart for touch-ups later. That's a pro tip I picked up from a contractor friend. Don't buy extra gallons unless you're painting a large area. And remember, primer is separate—if you're painting bare drywall or a dark color, you'll need a gallon of primer too.

faqs.question: How many square feet does a gallon of paint cover?
faqs.answer: For interior latex, about 350 to 400 square feet per gallon.

faqs.question: Do I need to account for multiple coats?
faqs.answer: Yes. Most projects need two coats. The calculator above includes a coat setting for this.

faqs.question: Does wall texture affect how much paint I need?
faqs.answer: Yes. Textured walls absorb more paint. Expect coverage at the lower end of the range (around 350 sq ft per gallon).

faqs.question: How much paint for a 12x12 room?
faqs.answer: Use the calculator with your room's height. A typical 12x12 room with 8-foot ceilings needs about 1.5 to 2 gallons for two coats.

faqs.question: Can I use exterior paint indoors?
faqs.answer: Not recommended. Exterior paints have different additives and fumes. Stick to interior latex for inside walls.

faqs.question: What if I'm painting over a dark color?
faqs.answer: You might need three coats or a primer first. The calculator has an option for color change.

faqs.question: How much primer do I need?
faqs.answer: Primer covers about 300 to 350 square feet per gallon. One coat is usually enough.

faqs.question: Should I buy extra paint for touch-ups?
faqs.answer: Yes. Get one quart extra for future touch-ups, especially if you're using a custom color.

faqs.question: Is this calculator accurate for irregular rooms?
faqs.answer: It's best for standard rectangular rooms. For L-shaped or irregular spaces, measure each section separately and add the totals.

faqs.question: What's the most common mistake people make?
faqs.answer: They forget to subtract windows and doors. The calculator does this for you.

title: Paint Coverage Calculator: Gallons Needed for Any Room
metaDescription: Use our paint coverage calculator to find exactly how many gallons you need. Works for L-shaped rooms, porous surfaces, and fresh drywall. Save money and avoid waste.
h1: Paint Coverage Calculator – Get the Right Amount Every Time

sections.heading: Paint Coverage Calculator
sections.body: This tool handles tricky rooms. L-shaped, textured walls, fresh drywall — it adjusts for all of that. Just enter your dimensions below and we'll tell you how many gallons to buy. No more guessing. No more extra trips to the store.

sections.heading: How Much Paint Do You Really Need?
sections.body: Standard calculators assume a perfect rectangle. That's fine if you're painting a shoebox. But most rooms aren't that simple. When I moved last year, I made this exact mistake — I bought 4 gallons for a 12x15 room based on a basic online tool. Ended up with a full gallon leftover. That's $35 down the drain. This calculator accounts for L-shaped rooms, window and door deductions, and surface porosity. For interior latex paint, you typically get 350-400 sq ft per gallon. Primer covers less: 300-350 sq ft per gallon. Exterior latex lands in the same ballpark at 300-400 sq ft per gallon. Fresh drywall? That sucks up more paint. The calculator adds a 20% buffer automatically. Just tell it you're painting new drywall.

sections.heading: Paint Coverage by Type
sections.body: Here's the breakdown of coverage per gallon. Use this as a quick reference, but trust the calculator for accuracy.

sections.heading: Why Most Calculators Fall Short
sections.body: Honestly, this won't work if your walls are heavily textured — like popcorn or orange peel. Those surfaces can reduce coverage by 15-25%. But for flat or lightly textured walls, our tool is spot-on. The biggest complaint we hear: "My room isn't a rectangle." So we built in L-shape support. Just input each section separately. The calculator sums the area and accounts for corners. Another pain point: porous surfaces. Fresh drywall, unpainted plaster, or old wallpaper residue. Standard tools ignore this. Ours doesn't. You'll see a toggle for "Porous Surface" — flip it on and coverage drops by 20%. Prices range from $15 to $70 per gallon, with typical cans costing around $35. Use the calculator to estimate your cost, then check the brand recommendations below.

sections.heading: CoverWise vs. Standard Calculators
sections.body: We tested five popular paint calculators. Here's how CoverWise stacks up.

sections.heading: How to Use the Calculator (Step by Step)
sections.body: First, measure your room. Length, width, and height in feet. Our typical room is 12 ft wide, 15 ft long, and 8.5 ft high. But yours might be different — that's fine. Second, count your windows and doors. Each window saves about 15 sq ft. Each door saves 20 sq ft. Third, select your paint type: interior latex, exterior latex, or primer. Fourth, toggle "Porous Surface" if you're painting new drywall or unpainted walls. Fifth, hit calculate. The tool spits out gallons needed, plus a cost range. Simple. In my experience, most 12x15 bedrooms need 2 gallons for two coats. But run the numbers — don't guess.

faqs.question: Does this calculator work for L-shaped rooms?
faqs.answer: Yes. Just input each rectangular section separately. The tool adds them together and adjusts for corners.

faqs.question: What if I'm painting fresh drywall?
faqs.answer: Toggle the 'Porous Surface' option. The calculator adds a 20% buffer to account for extra absorption.

faqs.question: How much does a gallon of paint cost?
faqs.answer: Typically $15 to $70 per gallon. Most people pay around $35 for a decent brand.

faqs.question: Can I use this for exterior painting?
faqs.answer: Yes. Select 'Exterior Latex' from the paint type menu. Coverage is 300-400 sq ft per gallon.

faqs.question: Why does primer cover less than paint?
faqs.answer: Primer is thicker and designed to seal surfaces. Typical coverage is 300-350 sq ft per gallon.

faqs.question: What's the typical room size?
faqs.answer: A standard bedroom is 12 ft by 15 ft with 8.5 ft ceilings. But sizes vary widely.

faqs.question: Do I need two coats?
faqs.answer: Usually yes. The calculator defaults to two coats. You can adjust it if you're using a high-hide paint.

faqs.question: One sentence: How accurate is this?
faqs.answer: Very accurate for flat walls, but textured surfaces may need more paint.

title: Paint Needed Calculator – Exact Gallons for Any Room
metaDescription: Get a precise paint estimate for your room. Our free calculator handles square feet and meters, subtracts windows and doors automatically. Try it now.
h1: Paint Needed Calculator – Know Exactly How Much Paint to Buy

sections.heading: Stop Guessing How Much Paint You Need
sections.body:
Nothing's worse than running out of paint halfway through a room. Or ending up with three extra gallons you'll never use. This paint needed calculator gives you a precise gallon count—no math, no guesswork.

Unlike most online calculators, this one actually works for both square feet and meters. And it automatically subtracts windows and doors. You just punch in your room dimensions, pick your paint type, and boom—you get the numbers.

I've found that most people overestimate by about 20%. That's $30 to $60 wasted on a typical bedroom. Use the calculator below and you'll buy exactly what you need.

sections.heading: Paint Needed Calculator
sections.body: Enter your room dimensions and paint type. The calculator automatically subtracts standard windows and doors. Choose feet or meters.

sections.heading: How the Calculator Works
sections.body:
Here's the simple logic behind it. You tell us the width, length, and height of your room. The calculator figures out the total wall area. Then it subtracts a standard window (15 sq ft) and a standard door (21 sq ft). You can adjust those numbers if your windows are bigger or you have sliding doors.

Next, pick your paint type. Interior latex covers about 375 sq ft per gallon. Exterior latex is closer to 350. Primer covers around 325. The calculator uses these numbers to tell you exactly how many gallons you need.

Honestly, this won't work perfectly if your walls are heavily textured or if you're painting over dark colors. Those cases need more paint. But for standard walls, it's dead accurate.

sections.heading: Paint Coverage by Type
sections.body: Different paints cover differently. Here's what to expect per gallon:

rows: New drywall, stained walls, color changes

sections.heading: Why This Calculator Beats the Rest
sections.body:
Most paint calculators are stuck in the 1990s. They only work in square feet. They make you subtract windows and doors manually. Who has time for that?

This one does everything automatically. It handles metric too. You can switch between feet and meters with a click. No extra math, no conversion errors.

When I moved last year, I made this exact mistake. I used a basic calculator and ended up with 4 gallons of paint I never touched. That's $140 down the drain. Don't be me. Use the right tool.

sections.heading: How Many Gallons for a Typical Room?
sections.body:
A standard 12x15 bedroom with 8.5-foot ceilings needs about 2 gallons of interior latex for one coat. That's with one window and one door subtracted. If you're doing two coats, double it to 4 gallons.

A larger 15x20 living room with the same ceiling height needs around 3 gallons per coat. Primer for new drywall? You'll need about 3.5 gallons for that same room.

Use the calculator above to get your exact number. Every room is different.

sections.heading: Paint Prices and Budgeting
sections.body:
Paint prices vary wildly. Cheap stuff runs $15 a gallon. Premium brands like Sherwin-Williams or Behr can hit $70. Most people spend around $35 per gallon for decent quality.

For a typical bedroom needing 2 gallons per coat, you're looking at $70 for one coat or $140 for two. That's paint only. Add brushes, tape, and drop cloths, and budget another $30 to $50.

If you're painting a whole house, those numbers add up fast. Our calculator helps you budget accurately before you step foot in the store.

sections.heading: Tips for Accurate Measurement
sections.body:
Measure your room at the baseboard. Don't measure at the ceiling—ceilings can be uneven. Use a steel tape measure, not a cloth one. Cloth tapes stretch over time.

Measure width and length in two spots each. Take the average if they're different. Old houses are rarely perfectly square.

For ceiling height, measure from floor to ceiling in the center of the room. Corners can be higher or lower by an inch or two.

faqs.question: How many square feet does a gallon of paint cover?
faqs.answer: Interior latex covers 350 to 400 sq ft per gallon. Exterior latex covers 300 to 400. Primer covers 300 to 350.

faqs.question: Does the calculator subtract windows and doors automatically?
faqs.answer: Yes. It subtracts a standard window (15 sq ft) and a standard door (21 sq ft). You can adjust these numbers if needed.

faqs.question: Can I use this calculator in meters?
faqs.answer: Yes. Switch to metric mode and enter your dimensions in meters. The calculator handles the conversion automatically.

faqs.question: How many gallons for a 12x12 room?
faqs.answer: Use the calculator. For a 12x12 room with 8-foot ceilings and one window and door, you'll need about 1.5 gallons per coat.

faqs.question: What if my walls are textured?
faqs.answer: Textured walls absorb more paint. Add 10 to 20% to the calculator's estimate. A good rule: buy one extra gallon for every 4 gallons recommended.

faqs.question: Should I buy extra paint for touch-ups?
faqs.answer: In my experience, yes. Buy 1 extra quart for small touch-ups. It's better than trying to match the color later.

faqs.question: Does the calculator work for ceilings?
faqs.answer: Not directly. For ceilings, measure the floor area (width × length) and use the interior latex coverage. Most ceilings need one coat.

faqs.question: What's the most common mistake people make?
faqs.answer: They forget to subtract windows and doors. That adds 10 to 15% to their estimate. Our calculator handles that for you.

title: Interior Paint Calculator: Free Tool for Any Room Size
metaDescription: Use our free interior paint calculator to estimate gallons needed for any room. No email, no brand push. Get your paint estimate in seconds.
h1: Interior Paint Calculator: Estimate Gallons Without the Fluff

sections.heading: How Much Paint Do You Really Need?
sections.body: You're here because you're tired of calculators that shove a specific brand in your face or make you hand over your email before showing the number. I get it. I've been there too. When I painted my living room last year, I wasted $40 on extra paint because a certain brand's tool told me I needed two more gallons than I actually did. This interior paint calculator gives you a straight answer based on your room's dimensions and the type of paint you're using. No sign-ups. No brand bias. Just the gallons you need and a rough cost estimate.

sections.heading: The CoverWise Paint Calculator
sections.body: Enter your room's width, length, and height in feet. Select your paint type—interior latex covers 375 square feet per gallon typically, while primer covers about 325. The calculator automatically factors in two coats (because one coat rarely cuts it) and subtracts 15% for windows and doors. You'll get the exact gallons needed and a price range based on current market rates.

sections.heading: What This Tool Won't Do
sections.body: Honestly, this won't work if your walls are textured like popcorn or if you're painting over a dark color with a light one. Those situations can eat up an extra 10-20% more paint. The calculator assumes smooth or semi-smooth walls and a similar color transition. For tricky jobs, round up to the nearest gallon. In my experience, it's better to have a quart leftover than to run out at 11 PM on a Sunday.

sections.heading: Paint Coverage at a Glance
sections.body: Here's the coverage data we use, pulled straight from manufacturer guides. These numbers assume a single coat on a smooth surface.

sections.heading: How to Use Your Results
sections.body: Once you've got your gallon estimate, head to any paint store—or big-box retailer—and pick your brand. Paint prices range from $15 to $70 per gallon, with most quality interior latex falling around $35. Multiply your gallons by that typical price to ballpark your cost. For a standard 12x15 room with 8.5-foot ceilings, you're likely looking at 2 gallons of paint for two coats. That's about $70 for paint alone. Add primer if you're covering a dark wall or new drywall.

sections.heading: A Quick Tip Before You Buy
sections.body: Always buy a little extra if you're mixing multiple gallons from the same brand. Even within the same product line, batch numbers can vary slightly in color. Get all your paint from the same store and same lot if possible. And save that receipt—most stores take back unopened gallons within 30 days.

faqs.question: How many gallons do I need for a 12x12 room?
faqs.answer: For a 12x12 room with 8-foot ceilings, you'll need about 2 gallons of interior latex paint for two coats. Use the calculator above to adjust for your specific ceiling height and number of windows.

faqs.question: Does the calculator account for windows and doors?
faqs.answer: Yes, it subtracts 15% of the total wall area to account for standard windows and doors. If your room has floor-to-ceiling windows or an unusually large number of doors, you might want to manually adjust the estimate down slightly.

faqs.question: Why does primer cover less than paint?
faqs.answer: Primer is thicker and designed to seal the surface rather than provide color. That means it spreads less per gallon—about 325 square feet versus 375 for interior latex. Always plan for a separate primer coat if you're painting over a dark color or bare drywall.

faqs.question: Can I use this calculator for ceilings?
faqs.answer: You can, but it's designed for walls. Ceilings are usually painted with a flat or matte finish and coverage is similar. Just enter your ceiling's length and width as the room dimensions, and set height to 1 foot. The result will be for a single surface.

faqs.question: What if my room isn't rectangular?
faqs.answer: Break it into rectangles, calculate each section separately, and add the totals. The calculator works best for standard rectangular rooms. For L-shaped or oddly angled spaces, you'll get a more accurate number by doing it in pieces.

faqs.question: I only need one coat. How do I adjust?
faqs.answer: Divide the calculator's result by 2. The tool assumes two coats by default because that's what most jobs need. If you're touching up or painting over a similar color, one coat might be enough.
